amendments.normalized_body: In `process_backend.rs` timeout handling (~line 1047-1061), after `tokio::time::timeout` fires, `spawn_background_reap` is called which invokes `force_kill()` then `wait()`. However, the `io_and_wait` future is never explicitly cancelled/dropped before calling `spawn_background_reap`. Since `io_and_wait` owns `stdin_future`, `stdout_future`, and `stderr_future` (which hold pipe handles), those futures are dropped when the `match` arm exits scope. On most platforms this is fine because dropping the pipe handles closes them, but there is a subtle race: `active_child.clone()` means the `ManagedChild` Arc is shared between the dropped `io_and_wait` future and the `spawn_background_reap` task. If the child process is still writing to stdout/stderr when the timeout fires, the background reap task may hang in `wait()` because the stdout/stderr pipe read ends haven't been fully closed yet (they're dropped in the caller's scope but the OS pipe may still be open from the child side). This is a pre-existing design but the new timeout path makes it exercisable. Consider explicitly dropping the pipe handles (stdout_handle, stderr_handle) before spawning the reap task, or at minimum documenting that the reap task may block until the child exits after SIGKILL.

The `backoff_for_attempt` function uses `2u32.saturating_pow(attempt.saturating_sub(1))` as the multiplier, then `self.backoff_base.saturating_mul(multiplier)`. When `attempt` is 0 (which shouldn't happen but isn't prevented by the type system), `saturating_sub(1)` wraps to `u32::MAX`, making `saturating_pow` return `u32::MAX`, and the resulting delay saturates to a massive duration before being capped. The cap at 60s saves this, but an `attempt == 0` would produce an unexpectedly large delay (60s) instead of 0s. Consider adding a guard: if `attempt == 0`, return `Duration::ZERO`.

The `OpenRouterBackendAdapter` returns `enforces_timeout() -> true`, but its timeout enforcement relies on `reqwest::Client::builder().timeout(request.timeout)`. The reqwest timeout is a *response* timeout (time to first byte or total), not identical to process-level kill semantics. If the server sends data slowly (streaming), reqwest's timeout may not fire. This means the service adds a 30s buffer (`request.timeout + 30s`) assuming the adapter will handle timeout at exactly `request.timeout`, but in streaming scenarios the adapter timeout may fire late or not at all. The service safety-net timeout at `request.timeout + 30s` would then be the actual enforcement, which is correct but the 30s buffer is wasted. This is a minor semantic inaccuracy — documenting the difference between reqwest timeout semantics and process-level SIGKILL timeout would clarify expectations.

src/adapters/tmux.rs:564-585 still turns every non-zero backend exit into `FailureClass::TransportFailure`. That means `execution.mode = "tmux"` does not get the new exit-127 handling that was added in src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1460-1469, so a fatal "command not found" failure is still retried in tmux mode. The acceptance criteria call out exit 127 as terminal, so the tmux adapter needs the same classification logic instead of hard-coding `TransportFailure`.

src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:223-228 now makes the service timeout the canonical timeout for tmux, but src/adapters/tmux.rs:519-598 only runs `prepared.cleanup()` / `prepared.finish()` after `wait_for_session_exit()` returns. When the service timeout fires, that future is aborted before those lines run, and src/adapters/tmux.rs:601-658 only cleans up the tmux session itself. As a result, Codex/OpenRouter tmux timeouts leak the `PreparedCommand` temp files described in src/adapters/process_backend.rs:55-90 and skip failed-artifact preservation entirely.

final_accepted_amendments.normalized_body: The `backoff_for_attempt` function uses `2u32.saturating_pow(attempt.saturating_sub(1))` as the multiplier, then `self.backoff_base.saturating_mul(multiplier)`. When `attempt` is 0 (which shouldn't happen but isn't prevented by the type system), `saturating_sub(1)` wraps to `u32::MAX`, making `saturating_pow` return `u32::MAX`, and the resulting delay saturates to a massive duration before being capped. The cap at 60s saves this, but an `attempt == 0` would produce an unexpectedly large delay (60s) instead of 0s. Consider adding a guard: if `attempt == 0`, return `Duration::ZERO`.

src/adapters/tmux.rs:564-585 still turns every non-zero backend exit into `FailureClass::TransportFailure`. That means `execution.mode = "tmux"` does not get the new exit-127 handling that was added in src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1460-1469, so a fatal "command not found" failure is still retried in tmux mode. The acceptance criteria call out exit 127 as terminal, so the tmux adapter needs the same classification logic instead of hard-coding `TransportFailure`.

src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:223-228 now makes the service timeout the canonical timeout for tmux, but src/adapters/tmux.rs:519-598 only runs `prepared.cleanup()` / `prepared.finish()` after `wait_for_session_exit()` returns. When the service timeout fires, that future is aborted before those lines run, and src/adapters/tmux.rs:601-658 only cleans up the tmux session itself. As a result, Codex/OpenRouter tmux timeouts leak the `PreparedCommand` temp files described in src/adapters/process_backend.rs:55-90 and skip failed-artifact preservation entirely.

payload.summary: Final review accepted 3 amendment(s); restart required.
